supported_fragment: Linux filesystem C functions represented by the versioned frontend-neutral IR and function-local CFG
Explicit protocol operations with configured object roles, return contracts, effects, compensations, handlers, accounting obligations, and legal exits
Exact, normalized, or unknown object identity with uncertainty propagated to ANALYSIS_UNKNOWN
Boolean or relational accounting obligations; arbitrary metadata arithmetic is unsupported
Configured callee roles, local call-result provenance, and bounded one-call effect summaries with argument or captured-result substitution; general interprocedural expansion is unsupported

families.title: Replay and recovery completion
families.description: Required replay steps, expected sentinels, recovery ownership, and exit outcome consistency.

families.title: Topology and membership rollback
families.description: Pointer, list-membership, and multi-object topology effects that require compensation or a valid owner on failure.

families.title: Retry and return provenance
families.description: Return values must describe the current attempt rather than an earlier failed attempt.

families.title: Accounting and reservation consistency
families.description: Boolean or relational obligations between pending metadata work and matching reservations or counters.

families.title: Transaction lifecycle
families.description: Begin, commit, abort, and ownership-transfer rules for transaction-scoped and non-transaction effects.

families.title: Allocation and deallocation lifecycle
families.description: Allocation, reservation, publication, rollback, and release obligations for metadata objects.

families.title: Namespace and orphan lifecycle
families.description: Directory membership, link count, orphan tracking, and failure-completion relationships.

families.title: Quota and reference accounting
families.description: Quota, reference count, and paired accounting updates associated with metadata mutations.

families.title: Deferred work ownership
families.description: Explicit transfer of incomplete work to recovery, background, or deferred handlers.

rules.title: Ext4 required replay failure must match the operation exit
rules.summary: A failed necessary ext4 replay step cannot reach a success exit unless the protocol identifies a legal retry or recovery transfer.
unsupported_semantics: persistent write ordering and crash consistency
unconfigured helper-body effects

sources.claim: The replay helpers expose operation-specific return values that control replay completion.
The same replay completion contract remains present in the v6.14 implementation.
The same replay completion contract remains present in the v7.1 implementation.

An independently reviewed mainline fix changed ext4_fc_replay_inode() from unconditional success to returning the current replay error; this directly corroborates required-step outcome propagation for the ext4 replay family.
sources.quoted_text: Additionally, make ext4_fc_replay_inode() propagate errors properly instead of always returning 0.

rules.title: XFS realtime summary-copy failure must match the exit
rules.summary: Realtime summary-copy completion is governed by the current helper return outcome and cannot silently convert failure to success.
unsupported_semantics: persistent write ordering and crash consistency
unconfigured helper-body effects

sources.claim: Summary-copy completion is represented by the helper return outcome.
The summary-copy return contract remains present in v6.14.
The summary-copy return contract remains present in v7.1.
The reviewed mainline fix states that xfs_rtcopy_summary() must return its error rather than unconditional success, exactly matching the registered outcome obligation.
sources.quoted_text: xfs_rtcopy_summary() should return the appropriate error code instead of always returning 0. The caller of this function which is xfs_growfs_rt_bmblock() is already handling the error.

rules.title: XFS rtgroup inode ensure must preserve expected sentinel semantics
rules.summary: The ensure operation must distinguish expected absence from fatal failure and successful completion.
supported_semantics: overlapping return guards with explicit priority
unsupported_semantics: sentinel meaning inferred without a configured contract
arbitrary caller-specific reinterpretation

sources.claim: The ensure helper distinguishes expected absence from fatal failure and successful completion.
The expected-sentinel contract remains present in v7.1.

The XFS maintainer applied the exact sentinel/error-propagation fix to xfs-linux for-next as commit b7e53968cb88; this is accepted maintainer evidence but is not represented as a Linus-mainline merge.
sources.quoted_text: Applied to for-next, thanks! [1/1] xfs: propagate errors from xfs_rtginode_load commit: b7e53968cb8882c2d276429ea8550848a4940874

rules.title: Relocation-root attachment must be completed or rolled back
rules.summary: A relocation root attached to global filesystem state must not remain without a legal owner after recovery failure.
unsupported_semantics: general pointer alias analysis
concurrent relocation behavior

sources.claim: Relocation recovery attaches and conditionally clears relocation-root state around failure paths.
The relocation-root completion relationship remains present in v6.14.
Btrfs maintainer David Sterba accepted the exact relocation-root failure cleanup patch into for-next; this confirms the obligation without claiming a Linus-mainline merge.

rules.title: Sprout device failure must resolve every topology effect
rules.summary: A failed device-add operation must compensate or transfer all topology, active-pointer, and list-membership effects for the matching objects.
supported_semantics: multiple object-bound pointer and membership effects
unsupported_semantics: complete callee summaries for all device helpers
concurrent device-list mutation

sources.claim: Device addition mutates seed/sprout topology, active device pointers, and device memberships with distinct failure cleanup paths.
The multi-effect device-add lifecycle remains present in v6.14.

A reviewed historical fix in the same btrfs_init_new_device() sprout path confirms that device-add can incorrectly mutate global mount state; it does not cover the registry's list, active-pointer, and fs_devices rollback effects, so the rule remains heuristic.
sources.quoted_text: The device add somewhat surprisingly clears the readonly bit on the sb

rules.title: Retry exit must report the current attempt
rules.summary: After a fallback or retry, the function exit must derive from the current attempt rather than preserve a stale earlier failure.
unsupported_semantics: retry state propagated through arbitrary helper layers
path feasibility requiring general symbolic execution

sources.claim: The extra-isize operation performs fallback attempts whose final return must represent the active attempt.
The fallback attempt and return-provenance relationship remains present in v6.14.
Ext4 maintainer Jan Kara independently confirmed the stale-error retry diagnosis and supplied Reviewed-by for the exact one-line repair.
sources.quoted_text: Indeed. Feel free to add: Reviewed-by: Jan Kara <jack@suse.cz>

rules.title: Pending chunk metadata requires a matching reservation
rules.summary: When activation reports pending chunk metadata work, the operation must establish the configured matching reservation before a successful exit.
supported_semantics: negative, zero, and positive return outcomes
unsupported_semantics: arbitrary reservation arithmetic
cross-transaction aggregation

sources.claim: Positive activation creates pending chunk metadata work that is paired with chunk metadata reservation.
The activation-to-reservation relationship remains present in v6.14.
The activation-to-reservation relationship remains present in v7.1.
Btrfs reviewer Johannes Thumshirn independently agreed with the missing reservation diagnosis and supplied Reviewed-by, while requesting cleanup and zoned fstests follow-up.

rules.title: XFS transactions must be committed, cancelled, or transferred
rules.summary: After successful transaction allocation, every reachable operation exit must close the same transaction object through commit, cancellation, or an explicit ownership transfer.
supported_semantics: call-site argument substitution for transaction identity
transaction OPEN, COMMITTED, and COMPENSATED states
success, failure, and ambiguous function exits
unsupported_semantics: recursive or unbounded interprocedural expansion
general alias analysis across stored transaction pointers
nested transaction ownership beyond one configured call boundary

sources.claim: The ACL mode update allocates a transaction through an output argument and closes the same transaction with xfs_trans_commit.
The same allocation-to-commit transaction lifecycle remains present in v6.14.
The same allocation-to-commit transaction lifecycle remains present in v7.1.

The official XFS design document defines the normal one-shot allocation-to-commit pattern and says commit releases joined resources; it does not specify all failure cancellation or ownership-transfer exits.
sources.quoted_text: In the code, a one-shot transaction pattern looks somewhat like this:: tp = xfs_trans_alloc(<reservation>) <lock items> <join item to transaction> <do modification> xfs_trans_commit(tp); As items are modified in the transaction, the dirty regions in those items are tracked via the transaction handle. Once the transaction is committed, all resources joined to it are released

sources.claim: The versioned XFS design document preserves the normal allocation-to-commit and resource-release contract, but not the complete failure-exit rule.
sources.quoted_text: In the code, a one-shot transaction pattern looks somewhat like this:: tp = xfs_trans_alloc(<reservation>) <lock items> <join item to transaction> <do modification> xfs_trans_commit(tp); As items are modified in the transaction, the dirty regions in those items are tracked via the transaction handle. Once the transaction is committed, all resources joined to it are released

sources.claim: The versioned XFS design document preserves the normal allocation-to-commit and resource-release contract, but not the complete failure-exit rule.
sources.quoted_text: In the code, a one-shot transaction pattern looks somewhat like this:: tp = xfs_trans_alloc(<reservation>) <lock items> <join item to transaction> <do modification> xfs_trans_commit(tp); As items are modified in the transaction, the dirty regions in those items are tracked via the transaction handle. Once the transaction is committed, all resources joined to it are released

rules.title: Ext4 journal handles must be stopped on every completed path
rules.summary: After ext4_journal_start returns a non-error handle, every reachable operation exit must stop that same handle or explicitly transfer its ownership.
supported_semantics: same-object OPEN and COMMITTED lifecycle states
unsupported_semantics: recursive or unbounded interprocedural expansion
general alias analysis across stored journal handles
nested journal ownership beyond one configured call boundary

sources.claim: JBD2 requires every transaction start to have a matching stop before the transaction leaves the update phase.
sources.quoted_text: jbd2_journal_stop() the same number of times as jbd2_journal_start() before the transaction is completed

sources.claim: The versioned JBD2 documentation preserves the matching start/stop contract.
sources.quoted_text: jbd2_journal_stop() the same number of times as jbd2_journal_start() before the transaction is completed

sources.claim: The versioned JBD2 documentation preserves the matching start/stop contract.
sources.quoted_text: jbd2_journal_stop() the same number of times as jbd2_journal_start() before the transaction is completed

sources.claim: Verity enablement captures the handle returned by ext4_journal_start and closes the same handle with ext4_journal_stop.
The same journal-handle lifecycle remains present in v6.14.
The same journal-handle lifecycle remains present in v7.1.

rules.title: Allocated Btrfs search paths must be released
rules.summary: After btrfs_alloc_path returns a non-null path, every reachable parent-lookup exit must release that same path with btrfs_free_path.
supported_semantics: bounded one-call allocation and release summaries
call-result substitution for allocated path identity
same-object release on success and failure paths
unsupported_semantics: automatic cleanup attributes and cleanup macros
general pointer alias analysis across stored paths
allocation publication or ownership transfer beyond one configured call boundary

sources.claim: Parent lookup allocates a Btrfs search path and releases the same object on normal and failure exits.
The explicit allocation-to-release lifecycle remains present in v6.14.
The explicit allocation-to-release lifecycle remains present in v7.1.

A historical mainline fix in the exact btrfs_get_parent() operation restructures all post-search exits so the allocated path is freed exactly once, independently corroborating the same-object path lifecycle obligation.
sources.quoted_text: Clean up btrfs_get_parent() a little more, fix a free-after-free bug

coverage_targets.rule_goal: Freeze unseen replay/recovery operations and versions after the current development rules are locked.
exit_criteria: at least one unseen operation or version per applicable filesystem
positive, legal-negative, and unknown cases are independently adjudicated
protocol revisions after test-label access are prohibited

coverage_targets.rule_goal: Model begin, commit, abort, and handler ownership without allowing transaction abort to close unrelated global effects.
exit_criteria: one evidence-backed protocol instance in at least two filesystems
abort/global-effect counterexample remains a violation or unknown
unseen legal and violating paths pass independent review

coverage_targets.rule_goal: Relate allocation or reservation to publication, rollback, and release of the same metadata object.
exit_criteria: object mismatch cannot close an effect
legal publication and rollback fixtures do not report candidates
unresolved aliasing is reported as ANALYSIS_UNKNOWN

coverage_targets.rule_goal: Model directory membership, link count, and orphan ownership as related but independently auditable effects.
exit_criteria: namespace and orphan effects cannot compensate each other implicitly
legal deferred cleanup has an explicit matching owner

coverage_targets.rule_goal: Check boolean and bounded relational quota or reference obligations without claiming general arithmetic proof.
exit_criteria: boolean obligations have positive, negative, and unknown fixtures
protocol cost and unknown coverage are reported

coverage_targets.rule_goal: Require explicit object-, scope-, and guard-matched ownership when work is delegated to recovery or background processing.
required_capabilities: owner and object substitution at call sites
exit_criteria: wrong-object and wrong-scope handlers do not close effects
legal ownership transfer is represented in the witness
